items.title: Inferring mind wandering from perceptual decision making
items.author: Wouter Kool
items.content: Mind wandering is typically measured using rare, disruptive thought probes. This study shows that trial-by-trial fluctuations in attentional states can be inferred from behavior alone using a biased perceptual task and computational modeling.

items.title: Narrative context shifts gaze from visual to semantic salience
items.author: Floris P. de Lange
items.content: This study tests how meaning shapes gaze in picture stories. Viewers fixate earlier and more on semantically informative objects in coherent image sequences, an effect captured by a language-model–based semantic saliency rather than visual salience.

items.title: Self-serving biases shape the relationship between future thinking and remembering of elections
items.author: Clare J. Rathbone
items.content: Three studies of EU, UK, and US elections show that self-serving bias moderates the relationship between future thinking and remembering. Event memories and memories for pre-election expectations are shaped by having won or lost the election.

items.title: Retention and transformation of internal experiences in autobiographical memory narratives
items.author: Hongmi Lee
items.content: Using repeated autobiographical recall, this study shows that internal thoughts and feelings are less stable over time than externally oriented details, yet their retention and stability contribute to the perceived importance of personal memories.

items.title: Learning alters salience and proactive attentional priority
items.author: Jan Theeuwes
items.content: This study shows that past experiences alter the basic processing of visual input, demonstrating that learning alters attentional processing in a similar way to what has previously been shown with endogenous and exogenous attention.

items.title: Youth, personality and collective victimhood distinguish support for radical climate action
items.author: Winnifred R. Louis
items.content: A 3-wave study of 1427 climate-action supporters tests predictors of conventional versus radical climate activism. Radical intentions were rare and linked most strongly to youth, personality and collective victimhood rather than ideology or efficacy.

items.title: Actively distancing from climate radicals improves public support for moderate climate activists
items.author: Sabine Pahl
items.content: Support for moderate climate activists’ benefits from the rise of radicals as moderates are perceived reasonable in comparison — but only when they clearly distance themselves. These ‘radical flank effects’ are attenuated if moderates endorse radicals.

items.title: We need to understand economic inequality from children’s perspectives
items.author: Katherine McAuliffe
items.content: Economic inequality is on the rise, and children will bear its burden. Yet children’s perspectives are rarely considered. We urgently need interdisciplinary research to better understand how inequality gets into children’s heads and under their skins, and to inform policies that center children’s lived experiences.
